title: Words Between Two Worlds
summary: A boy in Toronto translates for his grandmother and learns what he is really carrying.

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: Leo, the doctor is talking too fast. Tell me what he says. My English is small.

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: He says your knee is okay, Grandma. But you must walk every day. Slow and short walks.

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: Walk every day? At my age? Tell him my knee is old, like me.

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: Grandma, I can't say that. I have to say it nicely. He is a doctor.

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: You always make my words soft. When I am angry, you make me sound calm. Why?

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: Because if I say your real words, people think you are rude. But you are not rude. You are just honest.

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: So you do not only change my language. You change my face for them.

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: Maybe. But I also tell you the soft truth. The doctor said the knee may hurt for a long time. I did not want you to feel scared.

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: Ah. So you protect me too. We protect each other with words.

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: Yes. It is hard, Grandma. I am twelve. Some words I do not even know in your language.

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: You do more than a machine, Leo. A machine gives the words. You give the kindness between them.

annotations.meaningVi: Nói cho bà biết ông ấy nói gì
Tiếng Anh của bà ít ỏi
sự tử tế ở giữa những lời nói

grammarNotes.title: Imperatives for instructions
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mệnh lệnh bắt đầu bằng động từ nguyên thể để đưa ra chỉ dẫn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: We use imperatives to give instructions, as in "Tell me what he says" and "walk every day."

grammarNotes.title: must and have to for need
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'must' và 'have to' đều diễn tả sự cần thiết.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: "must" and "have to" show necessity, as in "you must walk every day" and "I have to say it nicely."

grammarNotes.title: because to give a reason
grammarNotes.bodyEn: "because" gives a reason, as in "Because if I say your real words, people think you are rude."

translationVi:
Grandma: Leo, bác sĩ nói nhanh quá. Cho bà biết ông ấy nói gì. Tiếng Anh của bà ít lắm.
Leo: Ông ấy nói đầu gối bà ổn rồi, bà ạ. Nhưng bà phải đi bộ mỗi ngày. Đi chậm và ngắn thôi.
Grandma: Đi bộ mỗi ngày? Ở tuổi của bà sao? Nói với ông ấy là đầu gối bà già rồi, như bà vậy.
Leo: Bà ơi, con không thể nói thế. Con phải nói cho lịch sự. Ông ấy là bác sĩ mà.
Grandma: Cháu luôn làm lời của bà dịu đi. Khi bà giận, cháu làm bà nghe có vẻ bình tĩnh. Tại sao vậy?
Leo: Vì nếu con nói đúng lời thật của bà, người ta sẽ nghĩ bà thô lỗ. Nhưng bà đâu có thô lỗ. Bà chỉ thật thà thôi.
Grandma: Vậy là cháu không chỉ đổi ngôn ngữ của bà. Cháu còn đổi cả gương mặt bà trước mắt họ.
Leo: Có lẽ vậy. Nhưng con cũng nói cho bà sự thật nhẹ nhàng. Bác sĩ nói đầu gối có thể đau một thời gian dài. Con không muốn bà thấy sợ.
Grandma: À. Vậy là cháu cũng bảo vệ bà. Bà cháu mình bảo vệ nhau bằng lời nói.
Leo: Vâng. Khó lắm bà ạ. Con mới mười hai tuổi. Có những từ con còn không biết trong tiếng của bà.
Grandma: Cháu làm nhiều hơn một cái máy đấy, Leo. Máy chỉ cho ra từ ngữ. Cháu cho sự tử tế ở giữa những lời nói.

questions.prompt: What is Leo doing for his grandmother?
questions.explanation: She says "Tell me what he says. My English is small."

questions.prompt: What does the doctor say about Grandma's knee?
options: It is okay but she must walk daily
questions.explanation: "your knee is okay... But you must walk every day."

questions.prompt: What does Grandma want Leo to tell the doctor?
options: That her knee is old, like her
questions.explanation: "Tell him my knee is old, like me."

questions.prompt: Why won't Leo say Grandma's exact words?
questions.explanation: "I have to say it nicely. He is a doctor."

questions.prompt: What does Leo do when Grandma is angry?
questions.explanation: "When I am angry, you make me sound calm."
questions.hint: Grandma asks why he does this.

questions.prompt: Why does Leo soften her words?
options: So people don't think she is rude
questions.explanation: "people think you are rude. But you are not rude. You are just honest."
"You are just honest" contrasts with seeming rude — she means she tells the truth.
questions.hint: The word right after 'You are just'.

questions.prompt: What hard news did the doctor give?
options: The knee may hurt for a long time
questions.explanation: "the knee may hurt for a long time."
questions.hint: Leo's line about the soft truth.

questions.prompt: Why did Leo not tell her the hard news at first?
options: He did not want her to feel scared
questions.explanation: "I did not want you to feel scared."
questions.hint: Same line as the soft truth.

questions.prompt: What does Grandma realize they do for each other?
options: They protect each other with words

questions.prompt: How does Leo feel about his job as translator?
options: It is hard, and he is only twelve
questions.explanation: "It is hard, Grandma. I am twelve."

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: A translator carries kindness, not just words
questions.explanation: "A machine gives the words. You give the kindness between them."
questions.hint: The last line holds the theme.

Grandma: Walk every day? At my age? Tell him my knee is {{b3}}, like me.
Leo: Grandma, I can't say that. I have to say it {{b4}}. He is a doctor.
Grandma: You always make my words {{b5}}. When I am angry, you make me sound calm. Why?
Leo: Because if I say your real words, people think you are {{b6}}. But you are not rude. You are just {{b7}}.
Grandma: So you do not only change my language. You change my face for them.
Leo: Maybe. But I also tell you the soft {{b8}}. The doctor said the knee may hurt for a long time. I did not want you to feel {{b9}}.
Grandma: Ah. So you protect me too. We {{b10}} each other with words.
Leo: Yes. It is hard, Grandma. I am {{b11}}. Some words I do not even know in your language.
Grandma: You do more than a machine, Leo. A machine gives the words. You give the {{b12}} between them.

blanks.explanation: "My English is small" means she knows little English.
"I did not want you to feel scared."

criticalThinkingQuestion: Grandma says Leo changes 'my face for them' when he softens her words. Is it right for a translator to make someone sound more polite than they really are?
